data.title: Sumitomo Mitsui: Many Ways To Win
data.description:
Things are looking up for Sumitomo Mitsui Financial Group (NYSE:SMFG), along with the rest of the Japanese megabanks. Not only because Japan's central bank, the BoJ ('Bank of Japan'), has finally begun normalizing rates but also because the country now has a hawkish new Prime Minister, Shigeru Ishiba, who unlike his predecessors and key opponent Sanae Takaichi, is in favor of further interest rate hikes from here.

Despite an increasingly favorable backdrop, the market has opted for a more cautious view of banking upside, as reflected in SMFG's book value discount. Hence, there's an opportunity here for patient investors willing to tide things out in the meantime. Adding to the appeal is the 'optionality' enabled by SMFG's balance sheet, from its growing excess capital position, accretive overseas ventures, and more shareholder-friendly capital allocation. All in all, a very favorable risk/reward into earnings next month.

Japan Gets a Hawkish New Leader…

Bank stocks like SMFG have generally underperformed the recent Japanese rally; understandably so, given interest rates have lingered at near-zero levels for longer than expected. External uncertainties around the yen carry trade, geopolitical tensions and elections have also added to the overhang on Japan's megabanks and will probably linger into US elections next month.

Domestically, on the other hand, Mr Ishiba's victory as the new leader of Japan's ruling Liberal Democratic Party (or 'LDP') clears up some uncertainties ahead of a general election later this month.

For context, this is, unlike his LDP predecessors, a candidate in favor of normalized interest rates (i.e., more BoJ hikes) and fiscal consolidation to address cost of living issues. So, policy-wise, with opinion polls pointing to Ishiba's LDP as the clear general election front-runner, a structural shift away from Japan's 'Abenomics' days looks to be on the cards. In sum, a clear win for bank investors.

…Paving the Way for More Policy Rate Hikes

As for the BoJ, policy 'normalization', which entails a transition from negative to zero and, eventually, positive interest rates, has begun. So far, though, the pace of 'normalization' has been very gradual. Note that since the BoJ reversed course in March and hiked another 25bps in July, financial market volatility has kept policymakers cautious ("we can afford to spend some time in making a policy decision").

Hence, the swaps market has taken its cues and, accordingly, priced in policy rates of up to 0.4% through the March 2025 meeting and approximately 0.5% through September next year. In line with this cautious view of future interest rate hikes, SMFG has yet to fully regain its July highs and still trades at a de-rated book multiple. In sum, a low bar with ample room to re-rate once interest rate expectations reverse after this month's general election.

P&L Momentum Keeps the Setup Interesting…

In the meantime, company-level data out of SMFG has been positive heading into next month's half-year report. Per commentary from this year's IR day, for instance, early fiscal Q2 is already benefiting from the same momentum seen in Q1, which saw the bank tracking well ahead of target on both underlying profits and return on equity.

External data is also tracking a lot better than the bank's full-year guidance assumptions, most notably a 140 USD/JPY exchange rate (vs. 150 currently) and a 0.1% policy rate (i.e., no additional rate hikes). The latter is key, as it allows SMFG, along with the other Japanese megabanks, to pass through higher short-term prime rates to variable-rate mortgages and loans. Recent news flow suggests this process has already begun sector-wide, so expect to see a boost to SMFG's reported interest income next month (each 10bps rate increase adds ~JPY40bn of interest income per FQ1 sensitivities).

In sum, even though management says they aren't being as conservative with guidance this time around ("based on the most like scenario as of this May, while targets of last year were based on more conservative assumptions"), there's ample room for SMFG to outperform its guidance bar.

…while Balance Sheet Adds Upside Levers

On the other side of the equation is SMFG's improving balance sheet. Most notably, its 'stickier' deposit base, which is, to some extent, thanks to the healthy competitive dynamics in Japan, where the focus has been on differentiation, rather than deposit rate competition.

In SMFG's case, its efforts to increase deposit 'stickiness' is centered on "Olive", a 'one-stop shop' spanning banking, payments, insurance, and brokerage services for retail customers. Per IR day disclosures, "Olive" traction has only gone from strength to strength, with yen deposits from new account openers accelerating to +135% YoY and new account openings well on track to hit management's 12m mid-term target.

The rest of SMFG's balance sheet offers investors ways to win as well. From its 15% stake in high-flying US investment bank Jefferies (JEF) (see Jefferies: A Warren Buffett Stock With Runway) to the unwinding of its cross-shareholdings, SMFG's capital allocation is clearly moving in the right direction.

Also worth noting is the bank's best-in-class capital ratio, which, as of Q2, stood at 10.3% on a Basel III finalized basis (vs. 10% target). In other words, there's capital available for more buyback announcements, if not at next month's earnings report, then later over the course of the year. Alternatively, higher dividends (vs. the current ~3% yield) may also be on the cards, particularly with the bank tracking well ahead of its targeted run rate.

Summing Up

SMFG, along with the rest of Japan's megabank stocks, haven't quite recovered since the volatility of Q3. As things stabilize internally and externally through year-end, however, the bank has a clear re-rating path from here. In the meantime, not a lot of SMFG's upside levers, either on the P&L side or the balance sheet, are adequately accounted for in management's (unchanged) mid-term targets, nor in the stock's stubborn discount to book. Hence, a great risk/reward for investors here while getting paid a ~3% yield to wait.

data.title: Lululemon Athletica: A Premium Brand Trading At A Discount
data.description:
I've always enjoyed working out and until recently, I didn't pay too much attention to the clothes I wore, to me, it was all about getting the exercise done. However, a few months ago, I got a pair of sweatpants from a company I had not heard a lot from, but they were some of the most comfortable pants I've ever worn. Stretchy and comfortable pants can make your workouts more enjoyable.

The company that produced the sweatpants was Lululemon Athletica Inc (NASDAQ:LULU). My friend, who got me the pants, was an ambassador for the brand, and ever since I've been keeping an eye on the company. Still, while I liked its products, I didn't like the price it was trading at. But lately, the high price and expectations the company had, have drastically come down (almost 47% from its previous high) and now present an attractive opportunity for many investors.

The Company

Lululemon was founded almost 25 years ago as a brand that targeted a specific niche, mainly people who enjoyed doing yoga and were looking for stretchy and comfy pants, which were not that common at the time. Since then, the company has expanded and now manufactures sports-related clothes as well as fashion clothes dedicated to the "Athleisure" style.

What separates the company from its competitors is the way it promotes and sells its products, rather than partnering with well-known athletes, Lululemon focuses on finding local brand ambassadors and creating experiences inside its stores, where from time to time you can expect to be welcomed by a friendly staff into a fitness class given by a local instructor. Has this strategy worked? The numbers speak for themselves as sales over the last 10 years have grown at a 19.54% CAGR and even its EBIT has grown at a similar pace. To put it into context in the last 10 years Nike's (NYSE:NKE) revenues have grown at a rate of 5.67%, nonetheless, it is important to consider that Nike is a more mature company than Lululemon.

While the company's revenues do look impressive what sets the company apart from its competitors is the high EBIT margin it has, even against the market leader (Nike) they have a superior advantage.

How is the company able to achieve such margins?

The first reason why Lululemon enjoys high margins is its ability to charge higher prices for its products as consumers value the quality and innovation they offer. As previously mentioned, the company tries to create more of a culture and positive experience around its brand, rather than just making it a mere transaction-based interaction when you interact with it.

Lululemon's high operating margin is also a product of its high gross margin, as it employs efficient inventory management that allows it to forecast demand and avoid inventory write-offs. Also, they are constantly looking for opportunities to improve margins, for instance, in 2023 gross margins increased by 290bp due to efficiencies in freight costs, rates went down and there was a shift away from air freight.

The company also enjoys a lean-vertical structure, selling mainly through two channels, online and in company-operated stores. This structure allows it to bypass the higher markups that wholesalers charge and have better control over the experience customers have. To put into perspective the benefits of selling online rather than relying on stores, we can take a look at the percentage of sales companies derive from e-commerce:

  • Lululemon: 45%
  • Nike: 26%
  • Adidas: 20%

In summary, Lululemon has been able to differentiate its business model from its competitors by targeting a niche in the market that values quality and comfort, as well as having a marketing program directed towards finding ambassadors and operating a light-cost structure focused on a direct-to-consumer approach. All of this has allowed the company to have superior margins and profitability in comparison to its competitors and even against its history, as can be seen in Seeking Alpha's profitability score of A.

The Industry

The current sports apparel market is worth around 211.57 billion and has a projected CAGR of 4.68% for the next 8 years. Thus, we are talking about a mature industry with growth projections just slightly above the World's GDP growth projections.

The industry, as acknowledged by the company, is highly competitive, doing Porter's 5 forces analysis we get the following results:

  • Competitive Rivals: High - The industry is highly competitive, with many businesses fighting for market share.
  • Entry Barriers: Low - New companies can enter the space with little capital, just look at new competitors attracting market share such as Alo Yoga and Gymshark.
  • Suppliers Power: Medium - While many companies have developed strong manufacturing processes with suppliers, the switching cost to a new supplier is relatively low and with emerging markets competing with low labor more switching could be expected in the future.
  • Consumer Power: High - Given the ample choices customers have due to the low switching costs in the industry, they can pressure companies to offer better pricing through discounts.
  • Threat of Substitutes: High - Many clothing brands, such as fast-fashion ones, offer sporty clothes at more affordable prices, especially for customers who are just looking for clothes that look good and provide the "Athleisure" style at half the price of traditional sports companies.

After the previous analysis, we can conclude that the industry is a highly competitive one and the enterprises that manage to get most of the market share need to be perceived by consumers as offering value through their recognized brands, providing companies with a moat, albeit a very narrow one.

Lululemon given the high margins compared to competitors, does indeed show signs of having a moat based on offering premium and quality products that allows it to charge higher prices, nonetheless, the industry's structure makes it difficult for it to be a wide moat.

The Value

Having analyzed the company and the business model, it is time to determine whether the price is trading below fair value.

Revenues

Normally, I would assume a growth rate that is close to the company's historical trend, however, given that we are dealing with a young growth company, I will instead focus on the total addressable market and the share Lululemon could achieve.

As mentioned previously, as of 2024, the size of the industry is $211.57 billion. Taking into account Lululemon's LTM revenues, this would give the company a market share of 4.7%. For my valuation, I assume it can reach a market share of 7.5%, which would entail the company significantly reducing the double-digit growth it achieved in the last decade. This reduction, to me, seems reasonable, as Lululemon is becoming a more mature company and, to keep having the strong competitive advantage, it needs to keep marketing itself as a premium brand, which limits the potential size it can reach.

For some, this slowing growth might be too low - and, perhaps, I agree - but I rather be on the conservative side. The company currently has a plan known as the Power of Three ×2 growth, aiming to double its men's business, double its digital business, and quadruple its international business by 2026. This strategy coupled with its growing international expansion (specifically in the EMEA and APAC regions) does make at least a single-digit growth possible.

Margins

I already discussed a lot about Lululemon's best-in-class EBIT margin and while I do expect them to keep having strong margins, I'd rather again be cautious and expect them to settle at levels close to 20%, a reduction of almost 15% from the LTM number. I mainly assume this because the industry is highly competitive, and sooner or later for the company to keep growing and staying competitive, some margin level would need to be sacrificed.

Tax rate and WACC

For the tax rate, I assume it reaches a global marginal rate of 25%.

For its cost of capital, I use a regression beta of 1.25, an implied ERP of 3.94%, a risk-free rate of 4.09%, and a spread of 0.60% over the risk-free rate for the little debt the company has. Putting all of these numbers into the WACC formula, I get a cost of capital of 8.80%. However, as the company approaches its terminal value I do expect it to be a more mature and stable business with more debt in its structure, giving them a lower cost of capital of 8%.

Reinvestment

The company has historically had on average a sales-to-capital ratio of 3.50x, but currently, they have expansion targets that would warrant more investments, so for the first five years I assume the ratio is 1.50x and ultimately settle for the remainder of the forecast period at 3.50x.

ROIC

The ROIC I get for the 10 years of my valuation is the result of the previous inputs, yielding an average number of 30%. However, after year 10 I forecast that it will drop to 15%, giving the company some competitive advantage still, but that will be reduced as more competitors enter the industry.

All the previous inputs are summarized in the image below. At a price of $283.71, the company seems to be undervalued by close to 24%, thus yielding an attractive entry point.

Risks

Competition Risk

The company operates in a very competitive industry where the only way to get a return above its cost of capital is by creating a strong brand, but in an industry with low barriers of entry, the advantage can quickly disappear.

Economic Risk

Sportswear clothing is a discretionary buy that can be severely impacted by an economic slowdown.

Brand Reputation

The company relies on its local ambassadors to promote its products, however, if some of them were to engage in questionable activities the brand could lose its appeal to customers.

Raising Costs

Inflationary pressures and raising wages could negatively affect margins.

Summary

Lululemon has been able to distance itself from its competitors by having a different marketing strategy, direct-to-consumer sales, efficient inventory management and overall focusing on delivering quality on its products that pushes customers to pay a premium price. The company might have had ambitious expectations that were reflected in its price, but now, those expectations might have come down more than warranted and investors should consider adding exposure at current levels.

data.title: Rates Spark: Markets Now Contemplating A 50bp December ECB Cut
data.description:
By Michiel Tukker and Benjamin Schroeder

ECB cut by 25bp and leaving dovish aftertaste

The European Central Bank cut the deposit rate by 25bp to 3.25% as expected, but what we did not expect was the overly dovish angle in the communication surrounding the decision. During the Q&A, ECB President Christine Lagarde highlighted that the decision was unanimous, which means that the earlier pushback by hawks did not materialise in an opposing vote. On the other hand, growth concerns seemed to be downplayed, yet this did not persuade markets into a more hawkish perception. Risks stemming from a Trump victory were also referenced, giving market doves another hook for reinforcing expectations for a December cut.

In fact, and to our surprise, markets now price in about a 25% chance of a 50bp cut in December. Markets have got ahead of themselves before, and based on the current economic data, we think the positioning is overly ambitious. Having said that, the catalysts to persuade rates markets away from a 50bp cut are hard to see as economic data is likely to continue softening for the time being. Add further downside inflation surprises, and markets may well see an ECB tempted to accelerate the trajectory back to neutral.

Now that traders are seriously contemplating 50bp cuts, the EUR curve could see further steepening from the front-end. Until we have clarity about Harris vs. Trump, more easing on the back of growth concerns will be the prevailing theme. The endpoint for the ECB seems to be relatively anchored for now, and thus, we’re talking about an acceleration of cuts rather than the number of cuts. The back-end of the curve remains more driven by global dynamics, reflected in the strong spillovers from the bear steepening of the UST curve on better-than-expected economic data.

Today’s events and market view

The ECB will release the survey of professional forecasters, but not much other data of relevance features on the calendar. We will watch out for the usual post-meeting flurry of commentary from ECB members, especially since the hawks appear to have fallen in line with Thursday’s decision. No speakers are officially scheduled, though.

The US will release housing start numbers, and a few Fed speakers, including Raphael Bostic, Christopher Waller and Neel Kashkari, fill the calendar for the day.

More attention will be paid to sovereign ratings over coming weeks. This Friday evening, the focus is on Italy with both S&P and Fitch reviewing their BBB ratings, both currently with stable outlooks. Sentiment appears to be geared towards the upside, underscoring an ongoing reordering within the semi-core and periphery eurozone sovereign bond space.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: AstroNova's Pricing Is Optimistic And Not An Opportunity
data.description:
AstroNova (NASDAQ:ALOT) is a printer manufacturer with a segment dedicated to labels (from small businesses to industrial use) and another one dedicated to specialized printers used inside planes.

The label printer business is competitive and cyclical and has a lower margin than the aircraft printer business. However, the former has the biggest capacity to grow long-term, compared to the latter, which is predicated on aircraft building recovery and then becoming a mature/declining business.

AstroNova's financial and capital allocation have been good over time, with maybe some excessive M&A activity this year, leading to more leverage than would be desirable, although still very manageable.

The name trades at a forward P/E ratio of about 18x. This multiple makes sense if the company can deliver operationally levered growth. However, if it can't (and both history and current business conditions do not help with this respect), then growing into the multiple will take a long time, and the price does not compensate for other risks (mainly recessionary in the label business). For that reason, I believe AstroNova is a Hold at these prices.

Company intro

Label printer segment: AstroNova's Product Identification business specializes in label printers. These range from small domestic-use printers (used by entrepreneurs and small businesses) sold under the brand Quick Label to much larger industrial-setting printers sold under the brand Trojan Label. Recently, the company acquired a company specializing in logistics and postal printing (Astro Machine, 2022) and another one specializing in packaging (MTEX NS, 2024). We will comment on these acquisitions later.

The segment generates about 2/3 of the company's revenues but only half of its profitability because it has a lower margin than the aircraft business. The machines do not seem particularly complex, and AstroNova is not a big player ($100 million revenues yearly in a market worth several dozen billion, at least).

The segment has a low margin (10/12% before overhead) and probably suffers from investment-driven cycles (like most machinery). On the other hand, it has a resilient characteristic driven by the consumption of supplies: after a printer sale is made, a recurrent stream of supplies will probably follow. Supplies make up about 30% of the company's revenue.

Aircraft printer segment: AstroNova's second segment is called Test & Measurement because the company started in the test-printer niche. However, that business' size today is undisclosed but probably inconsequential.

The T&M segment actually gets its business from selling aircraft printers. Here, the company is dominant (with contracts with Airbus, Boeing, Embraer, Gulf Stream, and the military) and enjoys higher operating margins (around 20/25%). The business is more stable (depends on aircraft construction and deliveries), but is also smaller in size. The number of planes built grows over time, but given that AstroNova already dominates, it doesn't have much to win above the market.

Historically low and cyclical margins: If we look at the company's public financial history, we can observe that they have been cyclical and that they have been relatively low (with an average of 4% on the operating side).

Ok capital allocation: AstroNova has not needed much CAPEX historically, ranging from 1/2% of revenues. The company has made several intelligent acquisitions so far. For example, the company acquired most of its competitors in the aircraft printer business, leading to a dominant position. Then, in 2022, it acquired Astro Machine for about $6 million (net of tangible assets acquired), and the business had generated almost $4 million in net income by the end of 2023.

Maybe more questionable is the company's latest acquisition of MTEX NS, for about $19 million, including earn outs and net of tangible assets, for a company that generated only EUR 3 million in EBITDA in FY22.

Leveraged but manageable: One of the questionable points of the MTEX NS acquisition is the increase in the company's debt, which pays a high interest of about SOFR (or EURIBOR) + 2%. This debt does not represent a large problem (its interest is about $3 million per year versus $11/12 million in operating income) but will take time to be repaid from net income.

Valuation

Business opportunity in labels: The labels segment probably has the best opportunity to grow long-term, based on the gigantic size of the broad label printing market versus on-plane printing. For example, AstroNova can grow into adjacent markets in label printing but not in on-plane printing.

Of course, the business is also heavily exposed to the business cycle. For example, in 2023, the label segment was flat vs 2022, despite the acquisition of Astro Machine in 2022 (FY23), which added $18 million in sales. This means that during 2023, the organic business shrunk by almost 20% and probably remains challenged.

Business opportunity aircraft: The aircraft printer business has a lower chance of growing fast over the long term simply because it needs the market to expand because it has occupied the whole category, whereas the label business can get share.

In the short term, many AstroNova bulls believe that the business could expand as much as 30% if global airplane deliveries (around 1,200 in 2023) reached pre-pandemic levels (about 1,600 in 2018). This is uncertain, given the gap is in a big part explained by supply chain disruptions and challenges at the second-largest builder, Boeing.

Operating leverage: Operating leverage is an essential driver of future profitability. Over time, AstroNova has shown cyclical but not particularly leverageable margins.

In the post-pandemic era, margins have expanded (see chart below, which includes some non-recurring factors in OpEx). However, the aircraft business recovered a lot in 2022/23 driving aggregate margins up. In the future, the label business has a higher chance of growing, and it will probably drive the company's aggregate margin down.

Current yields and expectations: The company is guiding revenues to grow MSD organically this year and for MTEX NS to deliver between $8/10 million in revenues. That would put yearly revenues for FY25 (calendar 2024) in the realm of $165 million. Applying EBITDA margins of 8% (the company guides for one or two points above, but I am not adding back SBC) and considering 1% of revenues in CAPEX, we could be talking of a cash-based operating income of $11.5 million. Removing $3.4 million in interest ($45 million by 7.5% interest) leads to about $8 million in pre-tax income, which after 25% taxes leads to about $6 million in net income.

Compared to this, the company's market cap is $107 million, meaning the company trades at an expected 18x P/E, or a low yield of only 5.6%, barely above Treasuries. Of course, the name trades at that valuation because shareholders expect growth. How much exactly depends a lot on operating leverage.

For example, with no operating leverage, for the company to deliver (what I would consider the minimum of) 12% yield, it should grow revenues to almost $300 million, or almost double what it is expected to generate in FY24 (this comes from applying a 7% post-CAPEX margin to $13 million in net income plus 25% taxes and $3.4 million in interest). This seems extremely optimistic. Even at a 10% growth rate, it would take 7 years.

On the other hand, with fixed OpEx (and assuming current gross margins of 37%), the company only needs to grow revenues 7% to deliver that 12% yield ($177 million in revenues with 37% gross margins and $45 million in OpEx). This does not sound so optimistic, but it actually is. It implies an aggregate operating margin of almost 12% compared to the current 7%. This seems unlikely, given the label business margins do not reach 12% even during good years.

Conclusion

AstroNova is an interesting business, but I believe it deserves to generate a sufficient return of about 12% before considering it fairly valued. The reason is that the name is still a microcap with low and cyclical margins, deriving most income and future growth from a competitive industry in which it is tiny.

The stock is far from providing that return today, probably because there is optimism about its future growth. Although that optimism might be somewhat justified, it seems exaggerated, as evidenced by the estimations above. The company should leverage its costs above management guidance, or grow its revenues 100% before generating a sufficient return.

If positive developments are already embedded in the stock price, then there are not a lot of fundamental upsides, unless one expects the market to become incrementally optimistic (playing a bigger fool game). I prefer situations where optimism is not priced in and where conservatism or even pessimism prevails. For that reason, I believe ALOT is not an opportunity at these prices, and rate the name as Hold.

data.title: ASML Holding: Why It's Headed Back To $1,000
data.description:
ASML Holding (NASDAQ:ASML) is a market leader that's trading at a good price right now -- 37% off its high. Actually, it's more than a market leader. It's a company that has a monopoly in the quickly growing extreme ultraviolet [EUV] lithography market. It has fallen due to a slowdown in the chip market, which has led the company to miss bookings estimates (really badly) and lower its Fiscal 2024 guidance. There are also regulatory risks involving China.

However, those are shorter-term concerns, and the long-term outlook is solid. Taiwan Semiconductor's (TSM) recent outlook for growing CapEx next year reaffirms this. The chip industry is cyclical, and slowdowns are bound to happen from time to time. For cyclical stocks, that can be the best time to buy.

When I see such a strong company trading at a reasonable price, I get excited. I also believe there's a good chance of ASML getting back to $1,000 per share (43.3% upside potential) within the next nine quarters. Therefore, ASML stock is a Strong Buy to me.

Two Reasons Why I Like ASML So Much

1. Limited Competition Leads To High Growth, Pricing Power, And Profitability

As stated above, ASML has a monopoly in the EUV market, which is expected to grow at a 20.1% CAGR from 2024-2031 (according to Data Bridge Market Research). It's the only company in the world with the capabilities of producing the EUV machines that are needed to manufacture highly advanced semiconductors.

It's also a dominant player in the deep ultraviolet (DUV) lithography market (which is used for manufacturing less advanced chips and is expected to grow at 8% per year until 2031). In the overall lithography market, it has more than 80% of the market share.

This means that it has immense pricing power, allowing it to charge hundreds of millions of euros for a single EUV machine. This competitive advantage is shown in its financial results, with its revenues and EPS swiftly trending higher over the years.

In addition, its EBITDA and net income margins come in at 33.8% and 26.4%, respectively, over the last 12 months, which are quite impressive, giving them high grades from Seeking Alpha. Further, ASML has been able to grow its gross profit margin over the years due to the lack of competition, as you can see below.

2. ASML Is Cyclical, But Not As Much As Some Big Chip Stocks

ASML is a cyclical company, and right now, it's going through a bit of a downturn. However, it's likely not as cyclical as companies like Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), Nvidia (NVDA), Intel (INTC), and Micron (MU), whose sales can fluctuate greatly based on short-term demand for electronics, PCs, and gaming equipment. This demand can be influenced by consumer trends and the macroeconomy.

Meanwhile, ASML sees declines when chipmakers cut back on CapEx. However, ASML still benefits from long-term trends like the increasing complexity of chips. As chips become more complex, companies will need to invest in ASML's EUV lithography machines. In other words, even when chip demand is low, chipmakers will continue investing in ASML's equipment in order to create new technologies and remain relevant.

Why Has ASML Stock Fallen?

I'm sure there have been countless articles on this already, so I'll keep it brief. ASML stock fell recently after reporting earnings that missed the mark big time regarding bookings, despite revenue and EPS beating projections. Q3 bookings fell to €2.63 billion, down 53% quarter-over-quarter, much lower than the Bloomberg consensus estimate of €5.39 billion.

This is due to customer cautiousness, as "it now appears the recovery is more gradual than previously expected," per ASML. As a result, its 2025 revenue guidance was lowered from €30-40 billion to €30-35 billion. Seeking Alpha analyst Michael Del Monte also said that the lowered guidance is "likely the result of the Dutch government tightening export restrictions to China, shrinking ASML's market for EUV Lithography."

ASML's Business Is Still Strong And Can Recover

Despite the lowered guidance for 2025, the midpoint of the revenue guidance still suggests ~16% year-over-year revenue growth compared to the full-year 2024 revenue guidance of €28 billion. Plus, for 2025, analysts expect 30.67% in EPS growth.

Looking further out, I see no reason why ASML won't keep growing quickly. In the Q3 earnings call, ASML's CEO said:

Now what I've also said in the introduction is when we look at the long term, when we listen to our peers, I think that the bullishness about the long term opportunity of this market is strong. We share that, which means that at some point of time, the need for more capacity will be there.

He also said this:

Despite some of the push outs we have discussed, we continue to prepare for a number of new fabs that are being built across the globe to address the future demand and needs of the industry. Those fabs are spread geographically, our strategy for our customer and our schedule to take our system.

We will therefore continue to build capacity in order to respond to the demand increase that we expect throughout the remainder of this decade.

The CEO's optimism was made more credible based on what Taiwan Semiconductor said today after its earnings report. TSM said it is very likely that its CapEx will increase in 2025 compared to 2024 as demand increases. This directly benefits ASML.

What About China?

Regarding China sales, some investors who only look at ASML on a surface level may be spooked when seeing that the country made up 47% of ASML's Q3 revenue (see the image below), considering that there are export restrictions to China. However, China sales will only make up 20% of ASML's total revenue next year, per management, normalizing to historical levels. The decline in China sales is also baked in to the 2025 guidance, meaning that a lot of the risk has been de-risked.

Additionally, just because ASML is not allowed to ship China its EUV machine doesn't mean that it can't generate revenue from China. It's still able to sell DUV machines to China and service the machines that are already there. Thus, revenue from China won't go to zero, and the long-term trends powering growth are still intact.

The Valuation Is Very Reasonable Now

After such a large drawdown and some risks already priced in, I like ASML's valuation. Based on its P/E ratio in the past decade, it's reasonable to say that a "fair" or average multiple for ASML stock is around 30x.

And yes, the P/E ratio is higher than that right now, but that's not my point. I think ASML can maintain around a 30x P/E ratio a couple of years from now. That doesn't seem like a high multiple for a high-growth monopolistic company. If we take the $33.48 EPS estimate from analysts for December 2026 and apply a 30x multiplier to that, you get a share price of $1,004.40, or about 43.36% upside potential 2.25 years from now. That gives you a 2.25-year CAGR of 17.36%.

Even to achieve a 10% CAGR, which most investors would find suitable, you'd need a P/E ratio of just 25.93x in 2026 or EPS of $28.94 by 2026 if using a 30x multiple. I think this gives investors a decent margin of safety.

What Are The Risks?

To briefly reiterate, there's the China risk. Perhaps the Dutch government can become more stringent in the future and not even allow DUV sales to China, which would obviously hurt ASML.

There's also the risk that the market can take even longer to recover than ASML expects it to, which will lead to more downward EPS and revenue revisions. Further, although the recent past suggests that 30x is a reasonable P/E ratio to expect, there's nothing stopping investors from bringing that P/E to the low 20s, as they have briefly in the past. These factors can really hurt my thesis and belief that ASML can generate double-digit annual returns over the next couple of years.

The Bottom Line On ASML Stock

Following the recent dip, ASML is a strong company with a competitive advantage that's trading at a reasonable valuation. Based on its historical P/E ratio and analysts' revised estimates, I think ASML can trade at around $1,000 per share by the time it reports its Fiscal 2026 results, giving investors a 17.36% CAGR. But even if it doesn't, due to the risks I outlined above, there's a decent margin of safety to give investors at least a 10% CAGR over that period, leaving me bullish on the stock.

And it's not just the next two years when ASML will see growth. Management is confident in the long-term potential of the market, and the EUV lithography market, in which it has no competition, is expected to grow rapidly.

As stated in my recent article on Kering (OTCPK:PPRUY), "It's better to buy a strong brand when it's out of favor than when it's firing on all cylinders -- similar to a cyclical company." That statement applies here. ASML is a cyclical company in a downturn, but an upswing is likely on the way. Buy for the recovery.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Carrefour: Turnaround Story Facing Short Term Risks
data.description:
Carrefour (CRRFY, CRERF) has been working on a turnaround story over the last years and the results are showing. Nevertheless the share price does not fully reflect this. The high dividend yield and potential share price appreciation over the longer term make this company an interesting candidate to invest in.

Company overview

Carrefour is a French multinational food retailer. The company may not be well-known in the States as the company ceased it’s US operations in 1993, but is a major player nevertheless, figure 1 shows the global presence.

Since the current CEO, Alexandre Bompard, took the helm in 2017, the company has been streamlined into its current form. In brief, the store count has increased from 12.300 to the current 15.000 and the core geographies reduced to eight by divesting from China and Taiwan. Whereas presence of Carrefour in North America is absent, there is a strong presence in Latin America where the company further solidified its position with the acquisition of Grupo BIG in 2022. Even so, Europe remains the core geography in terms of store count.

Performance

About 45% of turnover is realized in France, and a further 28% in the rest of Europe. This means Latin America accounts for about 27% of total turnover. This division of turnover stands in stark contrast to the operating income of which only 50 percent was realized in Europe during 1H24, see figure 2.

From the figure it is clear sales and recurring operating income are under pressure in Europe. What the company calls ‘price investments’ are required to maintain market share and must be offset by cost measures. The company has been successful doing so in France, but in the remainder of Europe these ‘price investments’ unfortunately led to a reduction in operating income.

So far the adverse retail environment led to Louis Delhaize divesting its French operations to Carrefour. So while the environment has a potential negative effect on performance, it simultaneously creates opportunities to leverage the strong balance sheet and scoop up stores of competitors.

In contrast to Europe, sales and operating income are rising in Latin America. Diving a bit deeper into the numbers it appears the increase is single-handedly driven by Brazil as the contribution from Argentina is rather flat. This can be considered an achievement given the high inflationary environment in Argentina.

In a move to maintain market share, Carrefour has imported its Brazilian cash-and-carry formula Atacadão to France. This formula has proven itself in Brazil and the company is trying to apply the same formula in France. All in all, Carrefour is working hard to maintain performance but price leadership remains paramount even though inflation in Europe is declining.

Strategy

Under CEO Bompard, focus was put on two core geographies (Europe and Latin America), cost savings and improved free cash flow. By the end of 2022 Carrefour presented a new strategy which further focuses on cost savings and improvement of free cash flow, see figure 3.

While these savings and cash flow improvements are the financial targets, the operational targets in the figure show how management aims to achieve the financial goals. Increasing the market share remains on top of the list and based on the phrasing of ‘sustained gains’ the company may take over some stores, like those from Louis Delhaize, but no major acquisitions are foreseen.

Furthermore, the sales of sustainable products and increase in private labels signals the company wants to cater to the needs of both premium shoppers and price-aware consumers. The increase in private labels however is especially important as the margins on these products are generally better.

Ratios

Divesting from the Far East, investing in Brazil and focusing on cost discipline already yielded results. As shown in figure 4 the company improved the ability to convert sales into free cash flow (FCF). Whereas this number was well below 1 percent in the previous decade, since 2020 the ratio doubled to 2 percent last year.

Still, there is room for improvement if a comparison is made to Ahold Delhaize (ADRNY, AHODF). In recent coverage it was noted Ahold converts 2.7 percent of sales into FCF whereas this number was above 3 percent several years ago. Applying this ratio of 2.7 percent to the ball-park figure of €80Bn in annual sales by Carrefour, the potential exists to increase free cash flow to a level exceeding €2Bn. The company likely will need a few more years to achieve such a number, but recent performance indicates the management team is on the right track. This was necessary as well as, in the article on AholdDelhaize, it was pointed out Carrefour was the worst performer of a peer group, see figure 5.

While the starting date for this comparison affects the numbers, the performance of stock has been abysmal over the last 15 years, see figure 6. The dividend has cushioned the blow when it comes to total returns, but given the direction of the stock markets since the GFC, shareholders have been left with a massive underperformance.

Shareholder returns

While the previous two figures are a poor introduction for a section on shareholder returns, past performance does not guarantee future result. Traditionally this phrase is used to warn of the inverse situation, i.e. good performance may not last. Yet, assessing the recent performance of Carrefour indicates shareholder returns are improving while the share price does not reflect this yet. To support this statement, the development of free cash flow versus dividend and buybacks is shown in figure 7.

Free cash flow topped the €1Bn mark in 2020 and continued its ascend in the following years. What’s more, shareholder returns rose above the €1Bn mark in 2021 as the company started to execute buybacks. As a further display of confidence the dividend is paid in cash rather than the share based dividend of the previous decade.

The combination of buybacks and share based dividend makes the total amount of shares outstanding is decreasing which gives confidence the envisaged dividend growth can be maintained, see figure 8.

The number of shares reduced by nearly 11 percent since it peaked in 2020 and the current 2024 buyback program of €0.7Bn will reduce the share count further to approximately 675 million. The share buybacks of recent years on average amounted to €0.7Bn, which equates to about 7% of the market capitalization at the current share price.

On top of this the dividend has been increased to €0.87 per share which generates a 5.8 percent dividend yield at the time of writing. Going forward management aims for a more modest 5 percent dividend growth per year, a number that outpaces inflation meaning wealth is accumulating.

For some reason, it seems the market is not yet fully valuing the turnaround story of Carrefour. The next figure shows the PE ratio of two other European retailers, namely Ahold Delhaize and Tesco (OTCPK:TSCDF). The selection here is more narrow than in figure 5 as the American retailers have PE ratios which are way above those of their European counterparts.

Either way, with Carrefour increasing returns and continuing the turnaround, it is not unlikely the PE ratio will move towards that of Ahold Delhaize. This implies the PE ration can move from 12.5 to a level of 15.5. For the share price this means upward potential towards a level of nearly €19, or US$20. This is a 14 percent price increase compared to the current level of US$17.53 (OTCPK:CRERF).

Risks

Based on the data presented so far the turnaround story seems to be good to be true; a near 6% dividend yield, annual buybacks of 7% of market cap and an upside of 14% in the share price. One would almost think this is a small company that hardly anyone has heard of instead of the largest listed French retailer.

The political environment in France lately has been tumultuous. For investors the main take-away was public finances are a point of attention again. The budget deficit is forecasted to reach 6 percent of GDP this year and as a result French Prime minister Michel Barnier presented an austerity plan that is unheard of in France. To put the gravity of this debt in perspective the following passage is taken from the referenced article:

France's debt is expected to rise to close to 115 percent of GDP next year, which compares with an EU debt target of 60 percent, and is the highest national debt to GDP ratio among EU countries besides Italy and Greece. In absolute terms, France's debt stood at over 3.2 trillion euros at the end of June, having risen by about one trillion since Macron took power in 2017.

The financial state of the country also shows to start in the performance as the 1H24 update showed the company is struggling to maintain performance in France, see figure 9. Put simply, sales are down and recurring operating income is supported by cost discipline and lease management initiatives. The problem here is that cost cutting cannot continue endlessly and the same holds for lease management. At a certain point there is nothing left to sale and lease back. So while these actions are supporting the current performance, there will be little left when the French economy takes a turn for the worse.

While a flailing economy imposes risks on the income side of the equation, there is more imminent threat on the expense side as large French multinationals will likely be taxed more heavily in an effort to reduce the budget deficit:

In an effort to avoid social unrest, a significant portion of the fiscal burden will fall on businesses, which are expected to contribute approximately €13.6 billion to the deficit reduction plan through taxes alone. This includes €8.5 billion via an "exceptional tax on the profits of large companies.

French politicians understand spending cuts and increased taxes will erode political support and hence they target large companies. Based on recent history, TotalEnergies (TTE) was hit with a solidarity tax when energy prices spiked, it is a near certainty higher taxes will be levied in the near future. Obviously, every euro that ends up in the coffers of the French state will not be reinvested in the business or returned to shareholders. My base case is the company will maintain the forecasted dividend growth, with the share buybacks being reduced when the government decides to claim a bigger piece of the proverbial pie.

Next to the French component, Carrefour depends heavily on the performance in the Brazilian and Argentinian market to support the recurring operating income. Without treating the state of affairs in both countries in detail, it can be concluded both countries have a less resilient economy than the United States. Revisiting aforementioned suggestion that the PE ratio of Carrefour may match that of Ahold Delhaize, the case can be made this will not happen as the latter has exposure to the US rather than the weaker economies of Argentina and Brazil. With the buybacks and stock price gains potentially not materializing only the dividend is left.

Conclusion

Under guidance of the current CEO Carrefour brought focus to the business and increased performance. This performance is reflected in improved cash flow based on which shareholder returns increased substantially over the last three years.

The bull case presents a 5.8% dividend yield, annual buybacks of 7% of market cap and an upside of 14% in the share price. However, taking short term risks into account my base case is the dividend will be maintained, but the share buybacks will reduce and the increase in PE ratio will not materialize in the short term.

Nevertheless, being a long term investor, I believe the short term risks present a buying opportunity. Therefore I intend to initiate a position when the share price moves towards €14.50 (US$15.50).

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Yamato Holdings: Not The E-Commerce Stock I Am Buying
data.description:
E-commerce offers significant growth opportunities for freight and logistics providers. Companies that, I believe, have positive prospects supported by e-commerce growth are Cargojet, a cargo airline playing a key role in express delivery in Canada, and InPost which is a Polish company setting up a network of automated parcel machines in various countries in Europe. Companies that are less optimized to fully benefit from e-commerce growth are companies that have built their business on a network utilized previously for delivery of mail, and those businesses also continue to experience earnings drag from lower mail volumes.

In this report, I will be adding Yamato Holdings (OTCPK:YATRY)(OTCPK:YATRF) to my coverage and assess whether the company's stock is a buy, hold or sell.

What Does Yamato Holdings Do?

Yamato Holdings' primary activity is the delivery of parcels in Japan, with individuals and e-commerce platforms and businesses being the customers. The company facilitates individual-to-individual deliveries, business-to-business and business-to-consumer deliveries through standard parcel delivery, express delivery and international shipping. Additionally, the company also provides refrigerated shipping for perishables. The company also provides logistics solutions, moving services, financial services for logistics, IT solutions for supply chain and delivery management and vehicle leasing and maintenance services. According to its integrated annual report, Yamato Holdings has a 47.5% domestic market share for parcel deliveries with a 100% coverage in Japan, making it a front-runner to capitalize on e-commerce growth.

Softer Consumer Spending Hits Yamato Holdings

While e-commerce growth is seen as a catalyst for growth of companies providing delivery solutions, the reality is that Yamato Holdings saw its revenues decline by ¥14.5 billion or 3.5%. This was driven by a combination of lower volumes and lower unit prices. Retail still saw revenues growing, but volumes were down 3.3%, partially offset by a 1.8% increase in parcel prices. In the corporate domain, the volumes grew 6.9%, but unit prices declined 2.1%. All in all, Yamato had expected the retail segment to perform better, but due to lower real wages and lower consuming spending, the sales were somewhat softer.

In the Contract Logistics business, revenues declined due to lower logistical support, while global logistical support drove the increase for the global business, with mobility solutions sales being slightly higher. On the profit side, we see that the decline in the express business and contract logistics almost fully translated to lower earnings with a decline of ¥15.7 billion yen putting the company in a loss position for the quarter driven by non-recurring COVID-19 shipments last year. If we look at the margins, we can also see that this is not a thick margin business. Last year the Q1 margins were 0.4% and Q1 25 (FY25 starts in March 2024) this year saw a 3.5% loss margin.

What Are The Risks And Opportunities For Yamato Holdings?

The obvious opportunity for Yamato Holdings is growth of e-commerce. The company has the largest market share on the parcel delivery market. However, beyond that generic bull thesis built on e-commerce growth, I believe the challenges might, in fact, be bigger. Yamato is the market leader in Japan and that gives them an advantage over competitors, but does not mean that competitors cannot take market share from Yamato Holdings. Furthermore, we do see that despite growing revenues and growing parcel volumes, the margins have been in downtrend and even at the highest levels the margins were between 4 and 6 percent. So, it is a business with thin margins. For FY25, the company expects margins of 2.8% which hardly impresses me.

Even as the market leader, Yamato Holdings has lower margins, and there are several reasons for that. The first one is that the Japanese domestic market for parcel delivery is a competitive one. Furthermore, the door-to-door delivery, which is the most popular way to deliver parcels in Japan, is labor-intensive, and going from door-to-door brings a lot of costs that are variable, such as fuel costs for vehicles. I believe that the concept of door-to-door deliveries is not a future-proof model and, therefore, Yamato Holdings should explore other options such as automated parcel lockers.

Yamato Holdings Has Upside But There Are Risks

To determine multi-year price targets The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener which uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions, and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For Yamato Holdings, analysts expect total EBITDA generation of around $3.5 billion between FY23 and FY27 which would indicate a CAGR of 2.4% while cash flow generation is expected to be $542 million. From a fundamental perspective, there seems to be significant upside and taking the most conservative valuation, which in this case is the peer group valuation, would provide 45% upside based on FY25 earnings. That seems like an extremely compelling investment case. However, I do believe that there is one big element that makes Yamato Holdings less attractive, and that is the fact that the company has extremely thin margins. Being active in a business field with high-growth prospects often is equated to high profits, but that is clearly not the case for Yamato Holdings. The company has thin margins. At best those margins have been 5.4% and in the past years the margins have been sliding. For FY25, it is expected that margins will tick up slightly, but it is still quite far away from the 4 to 5 percent margins and from what I am seeing, there is no clear path towards margin expansions.

We are already seeing that just with parcel volume growth, the margins are not expanding. When assessing businesses, what I am looking for is strong cost amortization, and it seems that despite growth in volumes, part of the growth is being offset by unit pricing and the business has a low gross margin. Those are not elements that make a business attractive in my view and as a result, while there is upside, I do currently have a hold rating for Yamato Holdings.

If you are still interested in purchasing Yamato Holdings, rather than investing in the OTC ticker YATRY representing one unsponsored ADS or YATRF representing one ordinary share, I would opt for the listing at the Tokyo Stock Exchange where it trades under the code 90640. The reason is that the OTC listings have low volumes that could make it more difficult to acquire or sell shares at desired quantities and at desired prices due to a lack of liquidity that could prevent proper price making.

Conclusion: Not Impressed With Yamato Holdings

While I do believe that companies that have exposure to e-commerce could see significant growth in the years ahead, I am not quite impressed with Yamato Holdings. The stock does offer some upside, but we have also seen that softer consumer spending leads to Yamato Holdings actually becoming loss making. Furthermore, the Japanese domestic market is a highly competitive one and the Takkyubin (door-to-door delivery) method to me seems to be outdated and a hard to scale model to continue expansion and efficient operations. That is why I consider the stock a hold despite the significant upside the stock has from a fundamental perspective.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Main Street Capital: More Gains From This High-Yielder
data.description:
Article Thesis

Main Street Capital (NYSE:MAIN) is a very high-quality Business Development Company that continues to see strong net asset value per share growth thanks to strong loan performance and accretive growth spending. With more special dividends being likely, Main Street Capital will continue to offer a nice high-single-digit dividend yield, and with net asset value per share continuing to rise, there is also ample potential for share price gains in the long run.

What Happened?

Main Street Capital recently announced its net asset value per share guidance for the third quarter, although this wasn't the company's Q3 earnings release yet -- that will happen in early November. Nevertheless, the net asset value per share guidance is a good indication of the company's performance in the most recent quarter.

Main Street Capital expects to report a net asset value per share (or book value per share) of $30.54 to $30.60 when it reports its actual Q3 results, with the guidance midpoint sitting at $30.57. That would represent a nice increase of 2.6% compared to the previous quarter -- net asset value stood at $29.80 at the end of the second quarter. A 2.6% quarter-to-quarter growth rate pencils out to an annualized growth rate of 11%, which is quite appealing.

Looking at Main Street Capital's past track record when it comes to growing net asset value per share over time, we see the following:

Over the last three years, Main Street Capital has grown its net asset value per share by 18%, or around 6% per year. The expected Q3 growth rate is thus above the norm for Main Street Capital. That being said, the 3-year growth rate is higher than the 10-year growth rate (which is around 4%); thus there seems to be a trend of accelerating book value per share growth. If the most recent quarterly growth rate is higher than the 3-year average, that is aligned with a trend of accelerating growth for this metric.

That being said, there is, of course, no guarantee that Main Street Capital will continue to see its book value per share growth accelerate, and the company's growth rate may even decline in the future.

Main Street Capital's Strong Execution

There are many Business Development Companies, but they are not created equal. Instead, there are vast differences when it comes to net asset value growth, earnings growth, dividend growth, portfolio risks, and so on.

I believe that Main Street Capital is one of the highest-quality BDCs investors can pick from. This is likely, at least in part, due to the fact that Main Street Capital is an internally managed BDC. Many BDCs are externally managed, such as Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL), which is managed by Blackstone (BX). External management is not necessarily a bad thing, since good -- and shareholder-friendly -- external managers exist (BX belongs to that group, I believe). But external management can be a problem when the external manager is more focused on growing assets at all costs in order to drive up management fees, as this can be a huge detriment to the shareholders of the externally managed BDC. When a BDC is managed internally, however, there is little risk of abuse, especially when management is well-aligned with the interests of common shareholders. Looking at Main Street Capital's insider ownership, we see the following:

Insiders own close to 5 million shares, with that number rising relatively consistently in recent years. As a percentage of MAIN's entire share count, insider ownership declined slightly in the recent past, but at 6% it is still pretty high. I thus believe that there is good alignment between management and shareholders -- after all, insiders have around $250 million in the company and have thus very good reasons to grow shareholder value over time. This fact likely helps explain MAIN's positive performance in the past and its steadily climbing net asset value per share -- other BDCs, where interests are less aligned (or not aligned at all), oftentimes fare worse. The fact that management is internal, and that insiders have good reasons to operate the company efficiently, due to their own holdings of MAIN shares, have resulted in MAIN having highly competitive operating costs:

We see that Main Street Capital's operating costs are much more attractive compared to its peers. I think that total costs, including non-cash compensation (shares issued to management and employees), is the more relevant metric compared to the pure cash cost, as share issuance still impacts shareholders due to the (small) dilution it causes. Using that metric, MAIN is twice as efficient as its BDC peers, and also way more efficient than the average bank.

Risks across a BDC's portfolio are an important factor when it comes to long-term share price appreciation and earnings growth. After all, too-high risks will result in hits to net asset value due to depreciation, and the BDC will not be able to offer strong special dividends if credit costs eat up too much profit.

Main Street Capital has a pretty strong portfolio. The BDC mostly invests in lower-middle-market companies that generate around $3 million to $20 million in EBITDA. These aren't huge companies, which is why they can't access debt markets easily -- this, in turn, allows MAIN to demand high interest rates. On the other side, the companies that MAIN funds are not unprofitable startups, either, which reduces credit risks. In total, Main Street Capital invests in a little less than 200 companies, providing solid diversification -- if a single portfolio company fails, that would not result in a huge hit to MAIN on a relative basis, as the average portfolio company makes up just 0.5% of assets. There is also diversification across geographic markets and across industries, with Professional Services and Machinery being the two biggest industries, at around 7% each. The geographic split across the US looks like this:

We see that weaknesses in parts of the US could be balanced out by results from other markets, although a countrywide and harsh recession would have a larger impact on MAIN. That being said, the company even continued to pay its dividends during the COVID pandemic and the Great Recession, so it is not especially vulnerable to economic downturns. That being said, special dividends would likely be lower, or zero, in a major recession.

Main Street Capital's average portfolio company has a 2.9x debt to EBITDA ratio, which is not especially low, but not very high, either. According to MAIN's data (see presentation linked above), the average interest coverage ratio (EBITDA to interest) is 2.4, which is very solid.

So far this year, MAIN's realized losses totaled $9 million, or around 0.2% of assets, while unrealized appreciation was way higher, at $64 million. The net result between realized and unrealized losses/gains is thus easily positive, with the same being true for the previous quarters in 2023.

MAIN: A Reliable Income Stock

Most companies make quarterly dividend payments, but Main Street Capital is one of a few that make monthly dividend payments. This can be an advantage for those who live off their dividends, such as retirees, as the income stream is less lumpy. At the current rate of $0.25 per share per month, Main Street Capital pays out $3 per year in regular dividends, which makes for a yield of around 6%. On top of that, Main Street Capital also makes special dividend payments, however, as its net investment income is substantially higher than $3 per share per year. Recently, MAIN has been spending $0.30 per share per quarter on special dividends, resulting in total dividend proceeds of $4.20 per year per share at the current rate, which makes for a yield of just above 8%. Some other BDCs offer higher dividend yields; thus MAIN is not the best choice for those who want to maximize their current income. But considering the fact that MAIN's dividend has grown very reliably in the past, and factoring in that MAIN's dividend looks pretty safe, Main Street Capital is still an attractive income stock, I believe.

No investment is risk-free, but MAIN looks relatively low-risk from a business perspective. The average portfolio company is healthy and MAIN is well-diversified. In a huge economic downturn, its results would still come under pressure, however, and investors would likely not get any special dividends. If an economic downturn were to happen that is even more severe than the Great Recession, MAIN may even cut its regular dividend, although I believe this is an unlikely scenario for the foreseeable future.

Right now, MAIN trades at 1.75x book value. This is a higher valuation compared to most peers, but MAIN also has a better track record and more reliable growth than most BDCs; thus a premium valuation seems justified. Factoring in the book value growth that MAIN experienced in Q3, according to recent guidance, MAIN also trades relatively in line with its historic valuation. While a better buying opportunity might materialize in the future, this 8%-yielding high-quality company looks like a very solid long-term dividend growth pick right here.

data.title: Vertex Pharmaceuticals: The Future Looks Bright, But It May Be A Choppy Ride
data.description:
Vertex (NASDAQ:VRTX) has a blossoming pipeline and continues to execute well. This article examines three commercial or nearly commercial products and the launch dynamics. While there may be volatility in share price, Vertex has a quality, maturing pipeline and a path to growth.

Casgevy

Vertex is currently launching Casgevy for sickle cell disease (SCD). This is a 60/40 program partnered with CRISPR Therapeutics (CRSP). Data suggests the treatment is functionally curative.

The company accurately describes the process as a “treatment journey.” The process is intense and prolonged— 8-10 months is possible. This month's long journey is challenging even for patients who are well resourced with an excellent support network. First, patients must consult with a provider at an Authorized Treatment Center. Patients must also wait for payers to establish eligibility and approve the substantial cost—$2.2 million per patient for the one-time treatment.

When treatment begins, blood stem cells are collected. The cells are sent to the lab where they are edited using CRISPR/Cas9 to correct the genetic defect, a process that can take several months. Once complete, the patient will undergo chemotherapy to destroy their defective cells, so the edited cells can engraft. Then, Casgevy is administered. The patient is hospitalized for this part of treatment.

The most burdensome facet of this complicated, multistep treatment process may be the chemotherapy. In addition to other complications, chemotherapy can impact fertility. Some patients may require fertility preservation counseling as well as interventions prior to treatment. This may take months and delay treatment.

Cantor Fitzgerald’s analysts have estimated the launch and manufacturing setup at $500 m this year. The cost of goods to manufacture is extremely high relative to off the shelf medicines, and considerably higher than most small molecules. There are skilled labor costs to train staff at ATCs. There are ongoing costs to coordinate each patient’s treatment with the sites who are cell harvesting, administering bisulfan pre-conditioning and then administering Casgevy. The margins Vertex realizes may be a metric investors wish to monitor. Of course, Vertex also has to engage in the customary negotiations with payers, which are particularly challenging given the economics of a large upfront payment for a costly treatment.

An additional issue that has emerged is that Medicaid and Medicare do not provide coverage for fertility preservation. Vertex cannot provide such services for patients at no cost as it could be construed as a kickback. Vertex has engaged in litigation to address this issue. It creates a conundrum for patients and also makes it difficult commercially. Without the availability of fertility preservation, some patients may opt to forgo treatment or wait until fertility coverage is available. It is estimated that approximately half of the SCD patients are insured by Medicaid.

Medicare and Medicaid have reimbursement, but it may be inadequate considering the intensive care providers need to deliver during the patient journey. Jennifer Klarer of The Dedham Group explained, “There’s a lot of uncertainty [among providers] around how much they’re going to be reimbursed and will it be enough,” “As you increase the cost of product, the degree of financial risk you’re taking on increases in parallel.” Commercially insured reimbursement rates may pose less risk to providers.

Meanwhile, bluebird bio (BLUE) launched competitive product Lyfgenia. It differs in that it delivers a healthy hemoglobin-producing gene using a viral envelope. The FDA included a blackbox warning in the prescribing information, alerting prescribers that hematologic malignancy has occurred in some treated patients. Monitoring is required. Bluebird priced the treatment at a considerable higher price ($3.1 m) than Casgevy ($2.2 m) despite the safety warning. ICER calculated the cost-effective price to be approximately $2m per patient.

To control costs, Bluebird has recently cut its workforce while engaged in the difficult task of ramping revenue as cash is rapidly depleted. The company has very limited cash on hand and shares change hands at approximately 50 cents, which may reflect concerns about the company’s ability to continue operations.

Vertex has advantages when competing with Bluebird. In my view, the medicine has superior pricing and a superior safety profile. Vertex has extensive commercial infrastructure and experience marketing in rare diseases. Given their highly profitable CF franchise, they have the ability to invest and play the long game, patiently waiting for a profitable franchise. Investors may wish to carefully monitor both launches.

Despite this incredibly burdensome and complex process, clinical trials for both treatments were oversubscribed. This underscores the extremely painful and debilitating nature of sickle cell disease and the limited efficacy of standard of care medicine hydroxyurea.

In September, Pfizer abruptly withdrew Oxbryta, an oral treatment for sickle cell disease, from the market. Data suggested it may increase patients' risk of death. In addition, data suggested treated patients may have more pain crises, the dreaded episodes that can lead to hospital visits.

The high fixed costs and logistical challenges make it difficult to see a clear, quick path to a profitable franchise for Vertex, but there is a true need for these functionally curative treatments.

Suzetrigine (VX-548)

Vertex reported that they will read out data from a ph 2 double-blind clinical study testing suzetrigine in a chronic pain indication— pain due to lumbosacral radiculopathy. Top-line data is expected in Q4. A 12-week study where patients receive 69 mg once daily (high dose in prior studies) or placebo is ongoing. The primary endpoint will be the change from baseline in the numeric pain rating scale.

While this is the first clinical trial of efficacy in this indication, it is notable that results in diabetic peripheral neuropathy (a different type of chronic pain) were positive. Assuming positive data in pain due to lumbosacral radiculopathy, there is a significant commercial opportunity as there are no approved medicines to treat this condition.

More broadly, chronic pain impacts an estimated 80 million patients and represents an area of considerable unmet medical need. It is well established that opioids are a poor option for chronic use, given their addictive potential. Other therapies such as gabapentin have limitations, including side effects at therapeutic doses and insufficient efficacy.

Suzetrigine has a PDUFA target action date of January 30, 202 in acute pain. This is a large market, with 1 billion days of acute pain medicine prescribed annually. Getting payer coverage for a branded drug in a market largely served by generics may be a challenge. VRTX will need to target hospitals and surgical centers as well as physicians' offices. Marketing will involve working with hospitals to gain formulary access through the Pharmacy & Therapeutics process. Launch dynamics may show faster uptake in the outpatient setting, as it may take additional time for inclusion in hospital-based formularies.

One of the more challenging endeavors investors in biotech and pharma face is estimating peak sales. Professional analysts are at times dramatically off base in their assessments. Highly anticipated launches routinely go much better or much worse than expected. Market research is critical in assessing a commercial opportunity. Research which clarifies the perspectives of prescribers and patients on the benefits and limitations of existing medicines and their receptiveness to new options helps shape launch expectations.

Vertex surveyed approximately 1000 adults who were treated for acute pain and 550 health care providers in the US who have prescribed medicines for acute pain. 71% of providers said they are in need of alternative medications to treat acute pain. 77% of patients said they are interested in trying a different pain medication in the future. 88% of providers prefer to manage pain without an opioid, with the risk of addiction being cited by 78% of respondents.

Ultimately, many of the drug launches which were highly successful have one common denominator—an unmet medical need for better therapeutics as perceived by prescribers and patients. This survey suggests both physicians and patients may be highly receptive to a non-opioid pain medicine such as suzetrigine

Vanzacaftor Triple - Vanzacaftor / Tezacaftor / Deutivacaftor

The new vanzacaftor triple for CF has a PDUFA date of January 2, 2025. It will likely be launched shortly thereafter. A priority review voucher was used to shorten the time from 10 to 6 months for FDA review.

The phase 3 data showed the new vanzacaftor triple to be non-inferior to Trikafta on the primary endpoint (ppFEV1) and superior on the secondary endpoint of sweat chloride. Doctors do not routinely measure sweat chloride in patients in clinical practice and some may consider Trikafta and the new vanzacaftor triple similar. However, patients benefit from once daily dosing. Additionally, some patients who could not tolerate Trikafta may be able to better tolerate the new vanzacaftor triple. Pricing is a key metric for investors to monitor, as is payer formulary and coverage policies.

Another issue for investors to monitor are the royalty payments Vertex makes to Royalty Pharma (RPRX). Royalty Pharma owns royalties on Trikafta and there is uncertainty about whether Vertex will owe a reduced royalty due to deutivacaftor, a component of the new triple being excluded from the royalty agreement. (Deutivacaftor is a deuterated version of ivacaftor which Vertex believes is excluded.) Legal action may be required to resolve this issue.

Financials/ Analyst Commentary

Analyst price targets range from $425 (RBC) to $598 (Goldman Sachs). Uncertainty on key variables that are inputs to the analyst’s models may account for the large difference in price targets. 2024 revenue guidance is $10.65 to $10.85 billion. The company had $10.2 billion at quarter end after the Alpine acquisition.

Conclusions

Vertex will likely have three launches ongoing in 2025. They each have unique challenges and involve substantial costs. The market’s expectations often clash with the reality that sales of most medicines take years to reach peak. A report by McKinsey & Co. noted that the sales figures in roughly two thirds of launches don't meet pre-launch expectations at year one. Perhaps this explains the classic "short the launch" mantra. Share price volatility often ensues as investors sort out what the actual sales trajectory looks like.

While Casgevy fits the profile of a transformational medicine for a true unmet medical need, investors may see volatility in share price as the realities of the launch become clear. In my view, it is a particularly challenging product to market.

The opportunity in chronic pain is critically important to Vertex, and suzetrigine may be their next blockbuster. The LSR data may move the share price as positive data would validate its utility in chronic pain, another large commercial market where there is significant unmet medical need. Critically, suzetrigine is a small molecule which is relatively simple to manufacture and dispense. The acute pain launch, in my view, is worth monitoring as there may be read through to a possible chronic pain launch.

While physicians may wish to try the new vanzacaftor triple in newly diagnosed CF patients, those who have not tolerated Trikafta or those who prefer once daily dosing, this is likely to bring a modest amount of additional revenue to the CF franchise. The royalty issue may ensnarl Vertex in litigation, but the outcome is very unlikely to be material to Vertex.

Looking forward, povetacicept in IgA nephropathy appears to be best in class. The phase 3 initiation is underway, but a launch is several years away if data is positive. VX-880 and VX-264, cell therapies for T1D, are advancing in the clinic and appear promising. There are competitors such as Seraxis (private) which seeks to develop implantable islet cells similar to those at Vertex. Vertex is ahead and has an assortment of technologies to utilize, including a proprietary device. Vertex is a profitable company with substantial financial resources and top tier in house scientific expertise. They are well positioned to maneuver through the scientific challenges that inevitably arise during drug development.
